CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized and presents contributions early (new symmetric collaborative setting, dataset, and DynoNet model), with a logical flow from method to experiments and conclusion. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables and examples effectively illustrate the dynamic KG and model behavior. Writing is generally clear and free of major grammatical issues, though the manuscript omits explicit discussion of limitations and code availability, which would improve reproducibility and clarity for readers. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and presents a logical progression from problem motivation through method (DynoNet) to experiments using the 11K human-human dataset and evaluations. The main contributions are stated early and the description of the evolving knowledge-graph, message-passing, and the encoder/decoder with attention and copy mechanism is clear; notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported. Figures, tables, and dialogue examples are informative and support the narrative, though the paper would benefit from a brief explicit discussion of limitations (omitted) and a pointer to code for reproducibility to improve clarity further.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized and presents contributions early (new symmetric collaborative setting, dataset, and DynoNet model), with a logical flow from method to experiments and conclusion. Writing is generally clear and free of major grammatical issues, though the manuscript omits explicit discussion of limitations and code availability, which would improve reproducibility and clarity for readers.

The main contributions are stated early and the description of the evolving knowledge-graph, message-passing, and the encoder/decoder with attention and copy mechanism is clear; notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported. Figures, tables, and dialogue examples are informative and support the narrative, though the paper would benefit from a brief explicit discussion of limitations (omitted) and a pointer to code for reproducibility to improve clarity further.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper states clear claims and provides a sizable human-human dataset (~11K dialogues) plus automatic and human evaluations showing DynoNet outperforms rule-based and neural baselines, which supports the main empirical claims. However, important experimental-detail gaps remain: code availability is not mentioned, baselines are not fully specified by name (EntNet only discussed), and there is no mention of statistical testing or seed control for reproducibility. The model description (dynamic KG embeddings, message passing, attention, copy mechanism) and reported hyperparameters indicate methodological soundness, so issues are minor and mainly concern experimental reproducibility and completeness of baseline comparisons. | B(4): The main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments and human evaluations showing DynoNet outperforms rule-based and baseline neural models on task success and human-likeness. There are no formal mathematical proofs to check, and the model description (message passing, entity abstraction, encoder/decoder with attention and copy) appears technically coherent. Some methodological details are unclear from the summary — e.g., extent of baseline coverage (EntNet comparison seems only discussed), presence of statistical significance testing, and release of code — which are minor gaps but do not undermine the core experimental evidence. Overall, the results justify the conclusions aside from these omitted controls and reporting details.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims and provides a sizable human-human dataset (~11K dialogues) plus automatic and human evaluations showing DynoNet outperforms rule-based and neural baselines, which supports the main empirical claims. However, important experimental-detail gaps remain: code availability is not mentioned, baselines are not fully specified by name (EntNet only discussed), and there is no mention of statistical testing or seed control for reproducibility. The model description (dynamic KG embeddings, message passing, attention, copy mechanism) and reported hyperparameters indicate methodological soundness, so issues are minor and mainly concern experimental reproducibility and completeness of baseline comparisons.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments and human evaluations showing DynoNet outperforms rule-based and baseline neural models on task success and human-likeness. There are no formal mathematical proofs to check, and the model description (message passing, entity abstraction, encoder/decoder with attention and copy) appears technically coherent. Some methodological details are unclear from the summary — e.g., extent of baseline coverage (EntNet comparison seems only discussed), presence of statistical significance testing, and release of code — which are minor gaps but do not undermine the core experimental evidence. Overall, the results justify the conclusions aside from these omitted controls and reporting details.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses an important and underexplored problem—symmetric collaborative dialogue with private KBs—and provides a substantial empirical contribution (≈11K human-human dialogues) that should reshape evaluations in this area. The proposed DynoNet model, which integrates evolving knowledge-graph embeddings with message passing, attention and copy mechanisms, demonstrates improved task success and human judgments compared to rule-based and neural baselines, suggesting researchers will adopt and extend these ideas. While not obviously transformative across all dialogue research (code availability is not mentioned and practical deployment constraints remain), the dataset and dynamic-KG modeling open clear new directions for symmetric multi-agent dialogue and grounded language modeling.  | B(4): The paper tackles an important and underexplored setting—symmetric collaborative dialogue with private KBs—and contributes a substantial resource (≈11K human-human dialogues) that should enable further study of grounded, symmetric interaction strategies. Its DynoNet approach, which integrates evolving knowledge-graph node embeddings, offers a clear inductive bias that meaningfully improves task performance and human-likeness over rule-based and baseline neural models, likely reshaping modeling choices for similar grounded dialogue tasks. While not evidently transformative across all dialogue research, the dataset and dynamic-KG modeling open promising research avenues and are likely to be adopted and extended by the community.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses an important and underexplored problem—symmetric collaborative dialogue with private KBs—and provides a substantial empirical contribution (≈11K human-human dialogues) that should reshape evaluations in this area. While not obviously transformative across all dialogue research (code availability is not mentioned and practical deployment constraints remain), the dataset and dynamic-KG modeling open clear new directions for symmetric multi-agent dialogue and grounded language modeling.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work falls squarely within the scope of a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it introduces a new collaborative dialogue formulation, releases a substantial human-human dataset (~11K dialogues), and proposes a novel neural architecture (DynoNet) operating over dynamic knowledge graphs. The methodology and evaluation meet community standards—comparison to rule-based and neural baselines, automatic and human partner evaluations (task success and multiple subjective criteria), and reported hyperparameters—making it relevant to researchers in dialogue, representation learning, and graph-based sequence models. From an audience and positioning perspective the paper addresses core interests of the venue (models, data, and evaluation for task-oriented dialogue) and provides enough empirical substance to merit presentation. | B(5): This paper fits very well within a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it tackles a timely problem in grounded, collaborative dialogue with a clearly motivated symmetric setting, introduces a substantial human-human dataset (~11K dialogues), and proposes a novel DynoNet architecture that integrates dynamic knowledge-graph embeddings with message passing and copy-based generation. The methods and evaluations match community expectations (automatic metrics and human partner judgments, comparisons to rule-based and neural baselines), and the formulation and dataset will be of clear interest to researchers in dialogue, grounded language, and representation learning. Overall the work addresses the right audience and is positioned appropriately for high-impact discussion in the venue.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents a sizeable human-human dataset (~11K dialogues), a detailed DynoNet architecture, and both automatic and human evaluations comparing to rule-based and neural baselines, which provides reasonable empirical support for core claims. However, the summary does not mention ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating contributions of message passing, entity abstraction, attention, and copy mechanisms, and no failure cases or limitations are discussed. Some reproducibility aspects are present (hyperparameters reported) but code availability is not mentioned, and important robustness analyses (e.g., seed variability, statistical significance) appear missing. | B(3): The paper presents substantial experiments (automatic and human evaluations, task success, comparisons to rule-based and neural baselines) and reports hyperparameters, which supports the main claims. However, the summary gives no evidence of ablation or sensitivity analyses for core modeling choices (e.g., message passing, entity abstraction, copy mechanism), nor discussion of failure cases or limitations. Related work coverage is said to be adequate, but code availability and full reproducibility details are not mentioned. Overall, the empirical evaluation is adequate but missing important analyses to fully validate the proposed inductive biases and generality.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a sizeable human-human dataset (~11K dialogues), a detailed DynoNet architecture, and both automatic and human evaluations comparing to rule-based and neural baselines, which provides reasonable empirical support for core claims. However, the summary does not mention ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating contributions of message passing, entity abstraction, attention, and copy mechanisms, and no failure cases or limitations are discussed. Some reproducibility aspects are present (hyperparameters reported) but code availability is not mentioned, and important robustness analyses (e.g., seed variability, statistical significance) appear missing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents substantial experiments (automatic and human evaluations, task success, comparisons to rule-based and neural baselines) and reports hyperparameters, which supports the main claims. However, the summary gives no evidence of ablation or sensitivity analyses for core modeling choices (e.g., message passing, entity abstraction, copy mechanism), nor discussion of failure cases or limitations. Related work coverage is said to be adequate, but code availability and full reproducibility details are not mentioned. Overall, the empirical evaluation is adequate but missing important analyses to fully validate the proposed inductive biases and generality.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable novelty by formalizing a symmetric collaborative dialogue task with private KBs and by collecting a large human-human dataset (~11K dialogues) tailored to that setting, which goes beyond commonly studied asymmetric task-oriented dialogues. The proposed DynoNet — dynamic knowledge-graph embeddings that evolve with dialogue, combined with message-passing, entity abstraction, attention and copy mechanisms — is a non-trivial methodological extension compared to prior sequence-only or static-KG approaches (e.g., EntNet). The authors also clearly position their contributions against related work and provide empirical comparisons, so an expert would find the ideas informative though not a radical departure from existing graph- and memory-based dialogue models. | B(4): The paper advances a notable and reasonably novel combination: framing symmetric collaborative dialogue with private KBs and collecting a large human-human dataset (~11K dialogs) tailored to that setting, and proposing DynoNet—dynamic knowledge-graph embeddings that evolve via message passing, entity abstraction, attention and copying. While the MutualFriends-style task itself is related to prior collaborative dialogue work (and EntNet-like methods exist), the explicit dynamic KG representation tied to generation and the dataset scale/analysis constitute a non-trivial extension beyond existing approaches. An expert would find the modeling perspective and dataset a meaningful, differentiated contribution, though not a radical departure from established techniques.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable novelty by formalizing a symmetric collaborative dialogue task with private KBs and by collecting a large human-human dataset (~11K dialogues) tailored to that setting, which goes beyond commonly studied asymmetric task-oriented dialogues. The proposed DynoNet — dynamic knowledge-graph embeddings that evolve with dialogue, combined with message-passing, entity abstraction, attention and copy mechanisms — is a non-trivial methodological extension compared to prior sequence-only or static-KG approaches (e.g., EntNet). The authors also clearly position their contributions against related work and provide empirical comparisons, so an expert would find the ideas informative though not a radical departure from existing graph- and memory-based dialogue models.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper advances a notable and reasonably novel combination: framing symmetric collaborative dialogue with private KBs and collecting a large human-human dataset (~11K dialogs) tailored to that setting, and proposing DynoNet—dynamic knowledge-graph embeddings that evolve via message passing, entity abstraction, attention and copying. While the MutualFriends-style task itself is related to prior collaborative dialogue work (and EntNet-like methods exist), the explicit dynamic KG representation tied to generation and the dataset scale/analysis constitute a non-trivial extension beyond existing approaches. An expert would find the modeling perspective and dataset a meaningful, differentiated contribution, though not a radical departure from established techniques.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates against a rule-based system and neural baselines on the same collected 11K-dialogue dataset with task success and human partner ratings, and reports training/dev loss and hyperparameters, which supports fairly controlled comparisons. However, stronger recent baselines (e.g., EntNet is only discussed in related work rather than empirically compared) and the lack of explicit naming/details for the neural baselines leave some important omissions. Overall the comparisons are good and largely fair but miss a few key competing methods and explicit baseline descriptions needed for a fully comprehensive evaluation. | B(4): The paper compares against a rule-based system and neural baselines and discusses related models such as EntNet, using the same collected MutualFriends-style data and a mix of automatic and human-partner evaluations, which supports fair head-to-head comparisons. However, the summary does not indicate inclusion of some stronger, recent end-to-end or memory-augmented dialogue baselines beyond EntNet, nor does it report explicit acknowledgement where prior methods match or exceed performance, so the comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates against a rule-based system and neural baselines on the same collected 11K-dialogue dataset with task success and human partner ratings, and reports training/dev loss and hyperparameters, which supports fairly controlled comparisons. However, stronger recent baselines (e.g., EntNet is only discussed in related work rather than empirically compared) and the lack of explicit naming/details for the neural baselines leave some important omissions. Overall the comparisons are good and largely fair but miss a few key competing methods and explicit baseline descriptions needed for a fully comprehensive evaluation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a rule-based system and neural baselines and discusses related models such as EntNet, using the same collected MutualFriends-style data and a mix of automatic and human-partner evaluations, which supports fair head-to-head comparisons. However, the summary does not indicate inclusion of some stronger, recent end-to-end or memory-augmented dialogue baselines beyond EntNet, nor does it report explicit acknowledgement where prior methods match or exceed performance, so the comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

main_claims: Introduces a symmetric collaborative dialogue setting where two agents with private KBs must identify a shared item via conversation.
Collected a large human-human dataset (~11K dialogues, MutualFriends-style scenarios) exhibiting diverse lexical, semantic, and strategic phenomena.

Proposes a neural model (DynoNet) using dynamic knowledge-graph embeddings that evolve with dialogue, combining message passing over KB nodes, entity abstraction, attention, and a copy mechanism for utterance generation.
Shows via automatic and human evaluations that their model is more effective at the task and judged more human-like than rule-based and baseline neural models.
_summary.methodology: Represent the agent's private KB and dialogue history as an evolving knowledge graph with item/attribute/entity nodes; compute contextual node embeddings via message passing and entity abstraction; use an encoder LSTM to embed incoming utterances and a decoder LSTM with attention over node embeddings and a copy mechanism to generate the next utterance.
datasets: Human-human collected dataset of 11K dialogues (collected via Amazon Mechanical Turk)
metrics: human partner evaluation ratings: fluency, correctness, cooperation, human-likeness
_summary.topic_area: Dialogue systems / task-oriented collaborative dialogue with dynamic knowledge graph embeddings

reviewer_a.name: Rigorous Experimentalist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with extensive experience building and evaluating neural NLP systems, particularly sequence models and graph-based architectures for dialogue and recommendation tasks.
traits: insists on clear, reproducible experimental protocols (hyperparameters, seed control, train/validation/test splits, and code availability)
looks for appropriate and strong baselines, ablation studies, and statistical significance when new models claim improvements
values detailed implementation and training details (model sizes, optimization, runtime, dataset preprocessing) to assess engineering plausibility and scalability
checks the validity of evaluation metrics and whether human evaluations are well-designed and statistically analyzed
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on methodological rigor: verify that experiments, comparisons, and ablations support the paper's empirical claims and that results are reproducible and robust.

reviewer_b.name: Situated Dialogue Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor specializing in dialogue systems and conversational AI, with a track record of work on task-oriented dialogue, emergent communication, grounded conversation, and dataset creation and analysis.
traits: evaluates novelty relative to prior work on collaborative/grounded dialogue and dynamic knowledge representations
attends to dataset design, annotation quality, and whether collected dialogues reflect realistic, diverse, and linguistically interesting phenomena
assesses broader impact and applicability: whether the setting addresses an important research gap and how transferable the approach is to other grounded dialogue tasks
notices conceptual clarity in problem formulation (e.g., symmetric collaboration), and whether the model's inductive biases align with the task goals
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Takes a high-level view of novelty and impact: judge how the paper advances understanding of collaborative grounded dialogue and whether the dataset and modeling contributions are significant and well-motivated.
